title: Integrals
blurb: Differentiation run in reverse, the area problem solved by slicing, and the Fundamental Theorem — the bridge that makes exact areas a two-step calculation.
buildsOn: Every derivative from Units 2–3 — you're about to run them all backwards.

lessons.title: Antiderivatives
lessons.concreteComparison: You know every 'child' each function produces when differentiated. Now the question flips: here's the child — who's the parent? That's antidifferentiation: reverse-engineering the function whose derivative you're holding. And since parents differing by a constant have identical children, the answer always ends in '+ C'.
lessons.objective: Find antiderivatives of powers and library functions, use the +C correctly, and pin down C from an initial condition.
concept: $F$ is an **antiderivative** of $f$ when $F' = f$. The power rule reverses cleanly: to undo 'exponent drops, steps down,' RAISE the exponent by one and DIVIDE by the new exponent: $\int x^n \, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$ (for $n \neq -1$). Check any antiderivative by differentiating — the check is always free.

The **+C**: since constants die under differentiation, $x^3$, $x^3 + 5$, and $x^3 - 99$ all have derivative $3x^2$. The antiderivative is a whole FAMILY of parallel curves, one per constant. Writing $\int 3x^2\,dx = x^3 + C$ names the entire family at once.

An **initial condition** picks one family member. If $F' = 2x$ and $F(1) = 5$: the family is $x^2 + C$; force $1 + C = 5$, so $C = 4$ and $F(x) = x^2 + 4$. The library reverses too: $\int \cos x\,dx = \sin x + C$, $\int \sin x \, dx = -\cos x + C$, $\int e^x dx = e^x + C$, $\int \frac{1}{x}dx = \ln|x| + C$ — the missing $n = -1$ case, solved by the log.
lessons.keyIdea: Antiderivative: raise the power, divide by the new power, add C — a family of parallel curves; an initial condition picks the one member.

facts.title: Reverse power rule
facts.explanation: Raise by one, divide by the new exponent — then + C.

facts.title: The family
facts.latex: F' = f \Rightarrow (F + C)' = f \text{ for every } C
facts.explanation: Constants vanish under d/dx, so answers come in families.

facts.title: Library in reverse
facts.explanation: The n = −1 gap is exactly where the logarithm lives.

workedExamples.solution: Term by term, raise-and-divide: $\frac{6x^3}{3} - \frac{4x^2}{2} + 5x + C = 2x^3 - 2x^2 + 5x + C$. Verify: $\frac{d}{dx}(2x^3 - 2x^2 + 5x + C) = 6x^2 - 4x + 5$ ✓. Differentiating your answer is the free, always-available check.
Family: $F(x) = x^3 - 2x + C$. Pin C: $F(2) = 8 - 4 + C = 7$, so $C = 3$. The one member through the point $(2, 7)$: $F(x) = x^3 - 2x + 3$.

commonMistakes.text: Dropping the +C — or attaching it to only some of a sum's terms.
commonMistakes.correction: Without $+C$ you've named ONE curve when the answer is a family — and initial-condition problems become unsolvable. One $+C$ per antiderivative (all the term constants merge into it), every time, until a condition pins it.

commonMistakes.text: Reversing the power rule as 'divide by the OLD exponent': ∫x³dx = x⁴/3.
commonMistakes.correction: Raise FIRST, then divide by the NEW exponent: $\int x^3 dx = \frac{x^4}{4} + C$. The check catches it instantly: $(x^4/3)' = \frac{4x^3}{3} \neq x^3$. Differentiate your answer — always.

tryIt.statement: Find $\int \left(4x^3 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}\right) dx$ and verify by differentiating.
tryIt.solution: Unmask: $x^{-2}$. Raise-and-divide: $x^4 + \frac{x^{-1}}{-1} + C = x^4 - \frac{1}{x} + C$. Verify: derivative is $4x^3 + \frac{1}{x^2}$ ✓.

tryIt.statement: A curve has slope $f'(x) = 2x + 1$ at every point and passes through $(1, 4)$. Find $f(x)$.

steps.title: Given the child, find the parent
steps.body: Three units of differentiation built a one-way dictionary: function → derivative. Half of calculus asks the REVERSE: here is $f$; find $F$ whose derivative is $f$. We call $F$ an **antiderivative**, write $\int f(x)\,dx$, and the payoff (two lessons from now) is enormous: antiderivatives compute exact areas.

steps.title: Raise, divide, add C
steps.body: To reverse 'drop the exponent, step down': **raise the exponent by one, divide by the new exponent.** $\int x^2 dx = \frac{x^3}{3} + C$. And that $+C$: differentiating kills constants, so $x^3$, $x^3 + 5$, $x^3 - 99$ are all legitimate parents of $3x^2$. The antiderivative is a FAMILY of vertically shifted curves; $+C$ names them all.

steps.title: Raise and divide
steps.explanation: Raise to 5, divide by 5. Option B differentiated instead of antidifferentiating; option C divided by the OLD exponent. Check by differentiating: $(\frac{x^5}{5})' = x^4$ ✓.

steps.title: Speed-dial, backwards
steps.body: Your four library derivatives flip into antiderivatives: $\int \cos x \, dx = \sin x + C$; $\int \sin x \, dx = -\cos x + C$ (check it: $(-\cos x)' = \sin x$ ✓); $\int e^x dx = e^x + C$. And the power rule's forbidden case $n = -1$ finally resolves: $\int \frac{1}{x} dx = \ln|x| + C$ — the logarithm was the missing parent all along.

steps.title: Reverse the wave
steps.explanation: Whose derivative is $\sin x$? Test $-\cos x$: $(-\cos x)' = -(-\sin x) = \sin x$ ✓. The minus signs on the wave pair trip everyone once — differentiate to confirm, every time.

steps.title: Initial conditions pin the C
steps.body: Real problems come with a known data point — the ball STARTS at height 2, the account OPENS at 1000. That **initial condition** selects one curve from the family: solve for $C$. Slope field $\to$ family $\to$ one curve through the given point. This tiny pattern is the seed of differential equations — most of physics is exactly this.

steps.title: Differentiate your answer
steps.body: Antidifferentiation has a luxury nothing else in math offers: a built-in, ten-second, complete correctness check. Differentiate what you wrote; if it isn't the integrand, fix it now. Never submit an antiderivative you haven't differentiated.

steps.title: What sticks
points: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C — raise, divide by the NEW exponent, add C.
The +C names a family; an initial condition picks the member.
Library reversed: cos→sin, sin→−cos, eˣ→eˣ, 1/x→ln|x| — and differentiate to check.

lessons.title: The Area Problem & the Definite Integral
lessons.concreteComparison: How do you measure a lake's area with only rectangular tiles? Tile it coarsely and you're obviously wrong; use thinner and thinner tiles and the error shrinks toward nothing. The definite integral is the tiling scheme taken to its limit — the exact area, defined as where the approximations are heading.
lessons.objective: Approximate areas with Riemann sums, define the definite integral as their limit, and interpret signed area.
concept: The area under $y = f(x)$ from $a$ to $b$ has no classical formula — curves aren't triangles. Plan: slice $[a, b]$ into $n$ strips of width $\Delta x$, erect a rectangle on each (height = $f$ at a sample point), and add: a **Riemann sum**. Crude for small $n$, better for large — and the error provably shrinks as slices thin.

The **definite integral** is the limit: $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum f(x_i)\,\Delta x$. It's a NUMBER (not a family of functions): the exact area under the curve between $a$ and $b$ — Unit 1's limit idea, deployed one final time.

Below the axis, rectangle 'heights' $f(x_i)$ are negative, so the integral counts that region NEGATIVE: the definite integral is **signed area** — area above the axis minus area below. $\int_0^2 (x - 2)\,dx = -2$: a triangle of area 2 that sits underwater.
lessons.keyIdea: Slice, sample, sum, and take the limit: ∫ₐᵇ f dx is the exact signed area — above the axis positive, below negative.

facts.title: Riemann sum
facts.explanation: Rectangles: sample height × width, added up.

facts.title: Definite integral
facts.explanation: The limit of the tiling — a number, not a family.

workedExamples.statement: Approximate the area under $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0, 2]$ with 4 right-endpoint rectangles, and compare with the exact value $\frac{8}{3}$.
workedExamples.solution: $\Delta x = \frac{1}{2}$; right endpoints $\frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}, 2$. Sum: $\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{4} + 1 + \frac{9}{4} + 4\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{15}{2} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75$. Exact: $\frac{8}{3} \approx 2.67$. The overshoot is the staircase poking above a rising curve — right endpoints overestimate; more slices shave it down.

workedExamples.statement: Compute $\int_0^4 x \, dx$ from geometry, and check that the integral agrees.
workedExamples.solution: The region under $y = x$ from 0 to 4 is a right triangle: legs 4 and 4, area $\frac{4 \cdot 4}{2} = 8$. Integral: $\int_0^4 x\,dx = 8$ ✓. When a region IS classical, geometry and the integral must agree — a standing sanity check for the whole theory.

commonMistakes.text: Treating the definite integral as always-positive area.
commonMistakes.correction: It's SIGNED area: regions below the axis count negative. $\int_0^2 (x-2)dx = -2$ even though the triangle has honest area 2. If a problem wants geometric area, split at the axis crossings and flip the negative pieces.

commonMistakes.text: Confusing ∫f(x)dx (a family of functions) with ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx (a number).
commonMistakes.correction: The indefinite integral is the antiderivative family ($+C$ and all); the DEFINITE integral has limits and is a single number — a signed area. Same symbol, two different species; the limits are the tell.

tryIt.statement: Approximate $\int_0^2 x^2\,dx$ with 4 LEFT-endpoint rectangles, and explain why your answer undershoots.
tryIt.solution: Left endpoints $0, \frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}$: sum $= \frac{1}{2}(0 + \frac{1}{4} + 1 + \frac{9}{4}) = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75 < \frac{8}{3}$. On a rising curve, left-endpoint rectangles tuck UNDER the graph — the staircase misses the wedges above each step.

tryIt.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (x - 2)\,dx$ and reconcile the sign with the picture.
tryIt.solution: The segment runs from $(0, -2)$ up to $(2, 0)$ — a triangle of geometric area $\frac{2 \cdot 2}{2} = 2$ lying entirely BELOW the axis. Signed: $\int_0^2 (x-2)dx = -2$. The integral reports the minus; geometry reports the magnitude.

steps.title: Curves have no area formula
steps.body: Calculus was built on two ancient problems. Tangents — solved, Units 2–4. The other: what's the AREA under a curve? Triangles and circles have formulas; the region under $y = x^2$ has none. The strategy that cracks it is pure Unit 1 thinking: approximate crudely, refine forever, and DEFINE the answer as the limit.

steps.title: Riemann sums
steps.body: Slice $[a, b]$ into $n$ equal strips of width $\Delta x = \frac{b-a}{n}$. On each strip stand a rectangle whose height is $f$ at a chosen sample point (left edge, right edge — either works in the limit). Add the areas: $\sum f(x_i) \Delta x$ — a **Riemann sum**. It's wrong, but honestly wrong: the error is visibly the little wedges between staircase and curve, and thinner slices shrink them.

steps.title: Watch the staircase melt
steps.teach: Step $n$ up: 2 slices are crude, 8 decent, 32 hug the curve. The sum readout climbs toward the true area — every extra slice shaves the error wedges thinner. The limit of this melting IS the definite integral.

steps.title: The definite integral
steps.body: $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum f(x_i)\,\Delta x$: the exact area, defined as the destination of the refining tilings. Note what species it is: a NUMBER. Not a function, not a family — one number for one curve over one interval. The elongated S is literally a stretched 'Sum'.

steps.title: Signed area
steps.body: What if the curve dips below the axis? The 'heights' $f(x_i)$ go NEGATIVE, so those rectangles subtract. The definite integral is **signed area**: above-axis regions count positive, below-axis negative, and they can cancel. Physics wants exactly this (a velocity below zero really does undo progress); geometry questions need you to split at the crossings and flip the minus pieces.

steps.title: Read the sign
steps.prompt: $f(x) = x - 2$ on $[0, 2]$ lies entirely below the x-axis, forming a triangle of area 2. What is $\int_0^2 (x-2)\,dx$?
steps.explanation: Below the axis, the integral counts negative: signed area $-2$. The geometric area is 2; the integral's job is the signed version — know which one a question is asking for.

steps.title: Archimedes got there first
steps.body: Around 250 BCE, Archimedes computed the exact area under a parabola by exhausting it with triangles — the same shrink-the-error idea as Riemann sums, two millennia early. He called it the 'method of exhaustion.' What he lacked wasn't the idea of integration; it was the SHORTCUT you learn next lesson.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Slice, sample, sum: Riemann sums approximate; thinner slices approximate better.
∫ₐᵇ f dx = the limit of the sums — one exact number.
It's SIGNED area: below-axis regions subtract; split at crossings for geometric area.

lessons.title: The Fundamental Theorem
lessons.concreteComparison: Your trip odometer doesn't add up thousands of tiny distance slices to tell you how far you've come — it just subtracts its reading at the start from its reading now. The Fundamental Theorem says areas work the same way: find an 'odometer' (antiderivative) for the curve, and any area is one subtraction.
lessons.objective: Evaluate definite integrals with the Fundamental Theorem: find an antiderivative, subtract its endpoint values.
concept: **The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:** if $F' = f$, then $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)$. Infinite rectangle-summing collapses to: antidifferentiate, evaluate at both ends, subtract. Notation: $\left[F(x)\right]_a^b$.

Why it's true: let $A(x)$ = the area accumulated from $a$ up to $x$. Nudge $x$ by a sliver $h$: the area gains a strip $\approx f(x) \cdot h$, so $A'(x) = f(x)$ — the area function IS an antiderivative of the curve. Any two antiderivatives differ by a constant, which the subtraction $F(b) - F(a)$ cancels — and so does the $+C$.

The theorem's name is earned: it welds the unit's two halves — derivatives (slopes) and integrals (areas) — into inverse operations. Slope of the area = the curve; area under the slope = the change. One theorem, both directions.
lessons.keyIdea: ∫ₐᵇ f dx = F(b) − F(a) for any antiderivative F — accumulate by subtracting the odometer at both ends; the +C cancels itself.

facts.title: Why: the area function
facts.explanation: Area grows at exactly the rate the curve is tall.

facts.title: No C needed
facts.explanation: The constant rides in and cancels out.

workedExamples.solution: Odometers: $x^3$ and $x^2$. First: $[x^3]_1^2 = 8 - 1 = 7$. Second: $[x^2]_0^5 = 25 - 0 = 25$. Compare with last lesson's infinite tilings: the same answers, in two lines each. That collapse is the theorem.

workedExamples.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos x \, dx$ and $\int_0^3 (x^2 - 4)\,dx$, interpreting the second result.
workedExamples.solution: Wave: $[\sin x]_0^{\pi/2} = 1 - 0 = 1$ — the first hump of cosine encloses exactly area 1. Second: $[\frac{x^3}{3} - 4x]_0^3 = (9 - 12) - 0 = -3$: net NEGATIVE — the below-axis part of $x^2 - 4$ (from 0 to 2) outweighs the above-axis part (2 to 3). FTC handles the signs automatically.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting in the wrong order: F(a) − F(b).
commonMistakes.correction: Upper limit FIRST: $F(b) - F(a)$. Backwards flips every sign — a positive area reports negative. Anchor it with the odometer: reading at the END minus reading at the START.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating only at the top limit: ∫₀³ … = F(3), forgetting F(0).
commonMistakes.correction: Even when $F(a) = 0$ it must be CHECKED, not assumed — $F(0) \neq 0$ whenever the antiderivative has non-vanishing terms (try $\int_0^1 \cos x\,dx$: $\sin 0 = 0$, fine; but $\int_0^1 e^x dx$: $e^0 = 1$, very much not 0). Two evaluations, one subtraction, always.

tryIt.solution: Odometer: $x^2 - 3x$. $[x^2 - 3x]_1^4 = (16 - 12) - (1 - 3) = 4 - (-2) = 6$. Watch the double negative at the lower end — the classic slip.
$[e^x]_0^1 = e - 1$. $[\ln x]_1^e = \ln e - \ln 1 = 1 - 0 = 1$ — the area under $\frac{1}{x}$ from 1 to $e$ is exactly 1 (that's one way to DEFINE $e$).

steps.title: Two problems, secretly one
steps.body: Slopes (Units 2–4) and areas (last lesson) look unrelated. The **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** says they're inverse operations: if $F' = f$, then $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)$. Every infinite tiling collapses to: find an antiderivative, evaluate at both ends, subtract. This equation is why the subject is ONE subject.

steps.title: The sweep argument
steps.teach: Watch the sweep: the filled area $A(x)$ grows exactly as fast as the curve is tall — gain a sliver of width $h$, gain area $\approx f(x) \cdot h$, so $A'(x) = f(x)$. The AREA function is an antiderivative of the curve. That's the theorem, in one animation.

steps.title: Run the odometer
steps.explanation: Odometer $x^2$: end reading $9$, start reading $1$, trip $= 9 - 1 = 8$. (Choosing $9$ means the lower limit got forgotten — the #1 FTC slip.)

steps.title: Geometry agrees, at every b
steps.teach: Step $b$: the region under $f(x) = x$ is a right triangle — geometry says $\frac{b \cdot b}{2}$. The antiderivative $\frac{x^2}{2}$ evaluated at $b$ says the same number, at every $b$. When two independent methods always agree, you're holding a theorem.

steps.title: Why no +C, and which F
steps.body: ANY antiderivative works: pick $F$ or $F + 17$ — the subtraction $F(b) - F(a)$ cancels the constant either way, which is why definite integrals never carry a $+C$. Practical protocol: antidifferentiate (skip the C), write $[\,\cdot\,]_a^b$, evaluate top, evaluate bottom, subtract — WITH the parentheses, because the lower value often arrives negative.

steps.title: A wave and a signed mix
The most important equation you'll write this year
steps.body: Mathematicians rank theorems half-jokingly by 'surprise per symbol.' The FTC is routinely voted near the top of all mathematics: eleven symbols connecting two ancient, apparently unrelated problems — and powering every physics engine, actuarial table, and rocket trajectory computed since 1687. You now own it.

steps.title: What sticks
points: ∫ₐᵇ f dx = F(b) − F(a): antidifferentiate, evaluate both ends, subtract (upper first).
Why: the accumulated-area function has A′ = f — area IS an antiderivative.
No +C in definite integrals — the subtraction cancels it.

lessons.title: Integration by Substitution
lessons.concreteComparison: Substitution is the chain rule watched in a mirror. Differentiating sin(x²) SPRAYS a 2x out front; integrating 2x·cos(x²) means spotting that spray and folding it back in. You're not learning a new move — you're learning to recognize the old one, reversed.
lessons.objective: Integrate composite expressions by substituting u for the inner function, including changing the limits of definite integrals.
concept: **Substitution** reverses the chain rule. When an integrand contains a composition AND (a constant multiple of) its inner function's derivative — $\int 2x(x^2 + 1)^3 dx$ has inner $u = x^2 + 1$ with $du = 2x\,dx$ sitting right there — rewrite everything in $u$: $\int u^3\,du = \frac{u^4}{4} + C = \frac{(x^2+1)^4}{4} + C$.

The recipe: (1) choose $u$ = the inner function; (2) compute $du = u'\,dx$; (3) swap until NO $x$ remains; (4) integrate in $u$; (5) substitute back. Off-by-constant mismatches are fine — $\int x \cos(x^2)dx$ has $du = 2x\,dx$ but only $x\,dx$ present: write $x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}du$ and carry the $\frac{1}{2}$.

For DEFINITE integrals, convert the limits too: $x$-limits become $u$-limits via the substitution, and you never return to $x$. $\int_0^1 2x(x^2+1)^3 dx$: $u$ runs $1 \to 2$, giving $\left[\frac{u^4}{4}\right]_1^2 = \frac{15}{4}$. Always verify antiderivatives by differentiating — the chain rule should regenerate the original integrand exactly.
lessons.keyIdea: Spot the inner function whose derivative is on scene, rename it u, swap completely, integrate, return — and definite integrals convert their limits along the way.

facts.title: The pattern
facts.explanation: Chain rule, reversed: the g′ on scene is the tell.

facts.title: Constant fixes
facts.explanation: Off-by-constant is fine; carry the fraction through.

facts.title: Definite version
facts.latex: x: a \to b \quad \Rightarrow \quad u: g(a) \to g(b)
facts.explanation: Convert the limits; never go back to x.

workedExamples.solution: Inner: $u = x^2 + 1$, and its derivative $2x$ is present: $du = 2x\,dx$. Swap: $\int u^3 du = \frac{u^4}{4} + C = \frac{(x^2+1)^4}{4} + C$. Verify: chain rule gives $\frac{4(x^2+1)^3}{4} \cdot 2x = 2x(x^2+1)^3$ ✓ — the spray folds back out.

$u = x^2 + 1$: at $x = 0$, $u = 1$; at $x = 1$, $u = 2$. Integral becomes $\int_1^2 u^3 du = \left[\frac{u^4}{4}\right]_1^2 = \frac{16 - 1}{4} = \frac{15}{4}$. New variable, new limits, no trip back to $x$.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving a stray x in the u-integral and integrating anyway.
commonMistakes.correction: The swap must be COMPLETE: if any $x$ survives (outside $u$), either express it via $u$ or the substitution is wrong — often the real problem needs a different $u$ or isn't a substitution problem at all. Mixed-variable integrals are meaningless.

commonMistakes.text: Keeping the old x-limits on the new u-integral.
commonMistakes.correction: $\int_0^1 \ldots dx \to \int_1^2 \ldots du$: the limits belong to the VARIABLE. Either convert them ($u$-limits) or substitute back to $x$ before evaluating — never evaluate a $u$-antiderivative at $x$-limits.

tryIt.statement: Evaluate $\int x \cos(x^2)\,dx$ (note the off-by-2) and verify by differentiating.
tryIt.solution: $u = x^2$, $du = 2x\,dx$, so $x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}du$: $\frac{1}{2}\int \cos u \, du = \frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2) + C$. Verify: $\frac{1}{2}\cos(x^2) \cdot 2x = x\cos(x^2)$ ✓.

steps.title: The chain rule left debris
steps.body: Raise-and-divide handles $x^n$; the library handles the classics. But $\int 2x(x^2+1)^3 dx$? That integrand is chain-rule DEBRIS: differentiating $(x^2+1)^4$ sprays a $2x$ out front, and here's that spray, lying at the scene. **Substitution** is the art of recognizing sprayed derivatives and folding them back in.

steps.title: Rename, swap, integrate, return
steps.body: (1) Set $u$ = the inner function — the thing wrapped by a power, root, wave, or exponential. (2) Compute $du = u'\,dx$ and CONFIRM $u'$ (up to a constant) is present in the integrand. (3) Swap until zero $x$'s remain. (4) Integrate the clean $u$-integral. (5) Substitute back. If step 2 fails — no $u'$ anywhere — substitution isn't your tool.

steps.title: Choose the u
steps.prompt: For $\displaystyle\int x^2 (x^3 + 7)^5\,dx$, what's the right substitution?
options: $u = x^3 + 7$ — its derivative $3x^2$ is present up to the constant 3
steps.explanation: The inner function is what's WRAPPED (here, by the 5th power), and its derivative $3x^2$ matches the $x^2$ on scene up to a constant: $x^2 dx = \frac{1}{3}du$. Result: $\frac{(x^3+7)^6}{18} + C$.

steps.title: The ½ that saves the day
steps.body: Perfect matches are rare; off-by-a-constant is the norm. $\int x\cos(x^2)dx$: want $du = 2x\,dx$, have only $x\,dx$. No problem — solve: $x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}du$, and the $\frac{1}{2}$ rides along outside: $\frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2) + C$. Constants can be imported and exported freely; missing FUNCTIONS cannot. $\int \cos(x^2)dx$ — no $x$ on scene — is beyond substitution (and beyond this course).

steps.title: New variable, new limits
steps.prompt: In $\displaystyle\int_0^2 x(x^2 + 1)^4 dx$ with $u = x^2 + 1$, what are the $u$-limits?
steps.explanation: Run the endpoints through the substitution: $u(0) = 1$, $u(2) = 5$. The old limits belonged to $x$; the new integral lives entirely in $u$-land.

steps.title: The tell is the derivative on scene
steps.body: Scan every integral with one question: 'is some function's derivative ALSO in the integrand?' $x$ next to $(x^2 + \ldots)$, $x^2$ next to $(x^3 + \ldots)$, $\cos x$ next to $(\sin x + \ldots)$, $\frac{1}{x}$ next to $\ln x$. That pairing is the substitution signature — see it, and $u$ chooses itself.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Substitution reverses the chain rule: u = inner function, du must be on scene (up to a constant).
Swap completely — zero x's — integrate, substitute back; differentiate to verify.
Definite integrals convert their limits: evaluate in u-land, never return.

practice.statement: Approximate $\int_0^4 x\,dx$ with 4 right-endpoint rectangles; compare with the exact (triangle) value.
practice.solution: $\Delta x = 1$, heights $1, 2, 3, 4$: sum 10. Exact: triangle area 8. Overestimate, as right endpoints on a rising line must be.

practice.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^{\pi} \sin x \, dx$ — the area of one full sine hump.
practice.solution: $[-\cos x]_0^{\pi} = -(-1) - (-1) = 2$. The famous result: each hump of the sine wave encloses exactly area 2.

practice.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (x^2 - 2x)\,dx$ and explain the sign of the answer.
practice.solution: $[\frac{x^3}{3} - x^2]_0^2 = \frac{8}{3} - 4 = -\frac{4}{3}$. Negative: on $(0, 2)$ the parabola runs BELOW the axis (roots at 0 and 2) — a genuine below-axis region, correctly counted negative.
$u = x^3 - 2$, $du = 3x^2 dx$ — a perfect match: $\int u^4 du = \frac{(x^3 - 2)^5}{5} + C$. Verify: $\frac{5(x^3-2)^4}{5} \cdot 3x^2$ ✓.

testYourself.statement: Find $\int \left(6\sqrt{x} - \dfrac{4}{x^2} + e^x\right) dx$ and verify by differentiating.
testYourself.solution: $6 \cdot \frac{2}{3}x^{3/2} + 4x^{-1} + e^x + C = 4x^{3/2} + \frac{4}{x} + e^x + C$. Derivative: $6\sqrt{x} - \frac{4}{x^2} + e^x$ ✓.

testYourself.statement: A particle's velocity is $v(t) = 6t^2 - 4t$ with position $s(0) = 3$. Find $s(t)$.

testYourself.solution: $[x^3 - x^2 + x]_{-1}^2 = (8 - 4 + 2) - (-1 - 1 - 1) = 6 + 3 = 9$. Parentheses on the lower value — it's a triple negative.

testYourself.statement: Compute the GEOMETRIC area between $f(x) = x^2 - 1$ and the x-axis on $[0, 2]$ (split at the crossing).
testYourself.solution: Root at $x = 1$. Below-axis piece: $\int_0^1 (x^2 - 1)dx = -\frac{2}{3}$ → area $\frac{2}{3}$. Above: $\int_1^2 (x^2-1)dx = \frac{4}{3}$. Total geometric area: $2$. (The raw integral $\frac{2}{3}$ would have hidden the cancellation.)

testYourself.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos x \, \sin^2 x \; dx$ (substitute the sine).
Evaluate $\int \dfrac{2x}{x^2 + 1}\,dx$, and explain how you recognized the form.
testYourself.solution: The numerator $2x$ is EXACTLY the derivative of the denominator $x^2 + 1$ — the substitution signature with $u$ = denominator: $\int \frac{du}{u} = \ln|u| + C = \ln(x^2 + 1) + C$ (absolute value optional: $x^2 + 1 > 0$). Verify: $\frac{2x}{x^2+1}$ ✓.
